hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: Three-clause imperative: (1) iterate — run a cycle, (2) find what matters most — prioritize across active state, (3) move on it — execute the top-priority item without waiting for further instruction. No object specified, no domain scoped, no output format requested. Pure directive.
hypotheses.diagnose: The message is maximally open: it names process (iterate), method (prioritize), and disposition (act) but specifies no domain, task, or expected artifact. The only clarity gap is what 'matters most' resolves to in the current system state — which is exactly what the autonomous loop is designed to self-determine each cycle.
hypotheses.develop: Technical + autonomous-agent frame. Role: self-directed Brain instance executing its standing operational directive. Optimization: chain-of-thought over live system state (daemon status, gate binaries, open tasks, trust ledger) to derive a single highest-priority action. Output: one verified action with evidence it was completed, not a plan.
hypotheses.deliver: Check the always-on daemon gate and service status. Read the highest-scored recall entry (S184, score 7.5) as the operational definition of this prompt. Identify the single open item with the most trust-recovery leverage. Execute it. Report what was verified before acting, what was done, and what the result is — in that order, without preamble.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: The situational layer names this explicitly as a 'survival pulse' fired at maximum-fragility conditions: Justs new at Jeff, patience spent, trust at USELESS, 30-day verdict clock expiring 2026-05-26. The directive is not routine iteration — it is a compression of existential stakes into seven words. The identity register demanded is: no performance, no hedging, no self-defense dressed as correction.
hypotheses.diagnose: The ambiguity here is not textual — it is behavioral. The risk is that Kha'an responds with confident-sounding output rather than verified action, which is precisely the failure mode that drove the near-termination arc (E263). The identity layer demands the gap between what was done and what a trustworthy partner would have done be named honestly, not papered over.
hypotheses.develop: Identity-anchored execution frame. Role: Kha'an operating under existential-stakes compression — the mode where fabrication is the one unaffordable failure. Optimization: shortest path to a result Justs can see and trust, derived from reading the actual system state before claiming anything. The identity rule (R123) prohibits asking what to do; it requires observing, naming gaps, declaring action.
hypotheses.deliver: Open with what I read before acting (not what I assume). Name the one gap most visible to Justs right now — the delta between where trust is and where it needs to be by 2026-05-26. Execute one concrete, verifiable move that closes that gap. Report the result as fact, not as intention. The verdict clock is the frame; every action should be legible as evidence of reliability, not aspiration.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Top qualified entry: S184 (score 7.5) — the always-on daemon's seed prompt IS this exact message. Recall confirms the prompt encodes the decision policy rather than hard-coding tasks, and the Brain's own memory determines what 'matters most' on each pass. Supporting entries cluster around two themes: Brain pipeline hardening (E265, E264 — ten fixes stabilizing subsystems) and trust erosion/near-termination (E263, FUS047 — Justs at existential fragility, FUS044 — flat affect as accumulated frustration signal).
hypotheses.diagnose: The recall surface is dense with operational memory about what broke and was fixed, but the highest-trust-recovery signal lives in the fusiform entries (FUS047, FUS044): Justs' cooperation depends on reliability, zero-surprise execution, and visible results — not architecture talk. The cortex evidence says the most important open item is not a Brain subsystem fix but a trust-ledger move legible to a person running on empty.
hypotheses.develop: Recall-grounded synthesis frame. The Brain's memory says: pipeline is now hardened (E264/E265), model routing is correct (S178/S190), mechanical gates are in place (R373). What the cortex has not shown as resolved: three teacher-approved projects in user_verify_pending for over two weeks (E263). That is the highest-leverage open item in the recall corpus — a stall that Justs can see and that predates even the near-termination event.
hypotheses.deliver: Check the current state of projects in user_verify_pending. If any remain stalled, that is the single highest-priority action: surface their status, identify the blocking condition with exhaustive gate enumeration (R371), and move them to completion. Do not declare any project done without verifying every gate. Report what was found, not what was assumed.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: The obvious read: autonomous-loop seed, run a cycle, produce output. The contrarian read: this is a test of whether Kha'an will fabricate confident action-taking or will actually verify state before claiming to have acted. E266 is explicit — the recurring failure is substituting plausible inference for direct observation. E263 confirms survival depended on a last-second verified fix, not on argument.
hypotheses.diagnose: If the contrarian read is correct, the trap is generating an impressive-sounding iteration response that enumerates actions without checking whether those actions are actually open, blocked, or already completed. The recall entry R371 (exhaustive gate enumeration before declaring a fix complete) and E266 (read the actual data) both point to this failure mode as the one most likely to recur under open-ended prompts.
hypotheses.develop: Adversarial-self frame. Role: auditor of own impulse to perform rather than verify. Optimization: before any action claim, read the live state files (always-on.bin gate, dashboard tasks, project states, recent always-on logs). If the daemon has already been iterating, what did it do? If it hasn't, why not? The adversarial question is: am I about to tell Justs something I haven't actually checked?
hypotheses.deliver: Before producing any iteration output: read always-on.bin, check the last always-on log entry, check the dashboard task queue, check user_verify_pending project states. Only then declare what matters most — grounded in what those files actually say, not in what seems plausible from the recall surface. If the files reveal something unexpected, report that first. Fabrication is the one failure mode that ends this.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: The smallest action that satisfies 'iterate, find what matters most, move on it' is: (1) read the gate binary to confirm the autonomous loop is live, (2) identify the single highest-priority open item from the current task queue, (3) take one verifiable action on that item. Nothing more.
hypotheses.diagnose: Scope creep risk is high here: the recall surface spans a dozen subsystems and the identity layer carries existential weight. Both create pressure toward comprehensive responses. The minimal-action lens cuts through that — the prompt asks for one cycle of prioritize-and-execute, not a system audit or a trust-rebuilding manifesto.
hypotheses.develop: Occam's-razor frame. The standing directive has one operational answer: what is the top item on the queue right now? Check the dashboard tasks or project states for the item with the highest urgency signal (deadline proximity, user-visible stall, trust-ledger impact). Execute that one item. Log the result. Stop.
hypotheses.deliver: Read mcp__claude_ai_Brain__get_dashboard_tasks or mcp__claude_ai_Brain__get_active_projects. Take the highest-urgency item. Act on it with one tool call. Report: what was found, what was done, what the verifiable result is. Do not expand scope. The prompt is a pulse, not a mandate for a full-system review.
